messages.content: You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: You have trapped a rival warlord's exhausted army in a narrow valley with no supplies. They will either surrender or starve, but your own forces are also running low on grain, and winter storms will close the pass in three days, threatening both sides with annihilation.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: The trapped army still has enough strength to inflict severe casualties if forced to fight a desperate last stand. | The rival warlord is known to be proud and unpredictable—he may see surrender as worse than death and choose mutual destruction. | Your own officers are divided: some want the glory of total victory, while others fear losing too many men so far from home.
ANGLES:
1) [FORCE MULTIPLICATION] Attack immediately at their weakest point with overwhelming concentration before the blizzard hits, accepting high casualties now to prevent starvation losses later.
2) [PSYCHOLOGICAL SUBVERSION] Leave the valley's far end visibly unguarded tonight and send a forged message suggesting reinforcements are coming—let them break out in disorder so you can defeat them piecemeal in open terrain.
3) [NEGOTIATED ABSORPTION] Offer to incorporate the enemy soldiers into your own ranks with full pay and honor, promising their commander safe exile rather than capture—turning their liability into your strength.
4) [STRATEGIC WITHDRAWAL] Withdraw your forces to the nearest garrison before the pass closes, sending a herald to offer the trapped army a truce until spring—preserving your army intact while leaving the rival indebted and weakened by winter.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: Your startup has just lost its biggest client (70% of revenue), but a rival's sudden scandal has opened a narrow window to poach their top three clients—doing so requires pulling your entire remaining team off core product work for six weeks and spending down your last 4 months of runway. Do you seize the opening or hold your position?

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: If you pursue the rival's clients and fail to convert them, your company dies with zero product progress and no cash. | If you stay the course on product, you buy 4 months to find new revenue—but the rival will recover and the window will close forever. | Your team is already stretched after the client loss; chasing this could cause key departures, while standing still might erode their confidence in leadership.
ANGLES:
1) [SEIZE WITH FULL COMMITMENT] Pursue all three clients simultaneously with your entire team for the full six weeks, betting the company on converting at least two. Accept the overextension as the price of survival.
2) [SEIZE WITH ECONOMY OF FORCE] Target only the one most convertible client using a small strike team, while the rest of the company continues product work. Capture what you can without abandoning your position.
3) [HOLD AND STRENGTHEN POSITION] Ignore the rival's clients entirely. Use the 4 months and full team to rapidly ship the product features your remaining clients need, aiming to grow organically and survive until new opportunities arise.
4) [SHAPE THE TERRAIN BEFORE ENGAGING] Approach the rival's clients not with a service pitch but with a strategic partnership proposal: co-invest in building the capacity to serve them properly. This defers immediate revenue capture but converts the opening into a larger, more sustainable position over 3-6 months.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: A border lord must decide whether to signal false weakness to lure a rival warlord into a decisive ambush, or negotiate openly for a truce that would spare both armies but risk appearing irresolute to his own captains.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: Intelligence is incomplete: the rival warlord's true strength and intentions remain uncertain, making both deception and open negotiation gambles on the same unknown. | Internal cohesion is fragile: the border lord's subordinate commanders are divided between those who see deception as dishonorable and those who see negotiation as cowardice. | The terrain favors ambush but the season favors delay: a battle now risks fighting in autumn mud, while a truce would push any conflict to frozen winter ground that nullifies the terrain advantage.
ANGLES:
1) [APPEAR WEAK TO DECEIVE] Publicly reduce patrols, spread rumors of low grain stores, and order a feigned retreat of your outer garrisons. When the rival warlord advances recklessly, spring the ambush from prepared high ground and annihilate his column before he can deploy.
2) [APPEAR STRONG TO DETER] Stage visible reinforcements by marching the same units repeatedly past scout positions, light extra campfires at night, and send boastful envoys demanding tribute. If the rival believes your strength overwhelming, he may withdraw without a fight or accept terms far more favorable to you.
3) [NEGOTIATE OPENLY FOR MUTUAL GAIN] Send a trusted emissary with full authority to propose a boundary settlement, winter grain-sharing pact, and a marriage alliance between houses. Accept that your captains will grumble, but preserve your forces intact and transform a rival into a buffer against the larger kingdom beyond the mountains.
4) [WITHDRAW TO SHIFT THE TERRAIN OF CONFLICT] Avoid both deception and immediate negotiation by pulling all forces back to a defensible river line deeper in your territory. Let the rival warlord occupy empty border forts that become a supply burden. Open negotiations only after his logistics stretch thin and winter forces him to choose between retreat and starvation.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: You are a senior engineer who just learned your company is secretly building a product that will make your current team's flagship project obsolete. Do you preemptively pitch a pivot to leadership before rivals in the firm leak it, or stay silent to avoid destabilizing your team mid-execution?

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: Any move you make could either secure your future or brand you as disloyal and paranoid before hard evidence surfaces. | Your team's morale and your own credibility are live ammunition—spend them too early and you lose the war for influence. | The rival division's timeline is uncertain; waiting clarifies the threat but may cede the only window to shape the outcome.
ANGLES:
1) [First-Mover Gambit: Shape the Field] Quietly approach leadership with a concrete integration proposal that folds your team's assets into the new product, framing it as a synergistic evolution rather than a retreat. Execute this meeting before the other division's narrative hardens.
2) [Bait and Observe: Let the Rival Commit First] Pretend ignorance and wait for the rival division to formally present their product. Their specific claims will reveal gaps and overreaches you can precisely counter with your team's proven execution record, turning their momentum into overextension.
3) [Fortify the Homeland: Consolidate Defensible Terrain] Immediately double down on deepening key client relationships and unique tech integrations that the new product cannot replicate quickly. Make your project indispensable on a dimension the rival cannot attack without your cooperation, forcing a merger on your terms.
4) [Controlled Revelation: Arm the Allies] Confidentially inform a trusted skip-level executive who outranks the rival sponsor, presenting it as a strategic observation rather than a complaint. Seed the idea that any transition needs your operational knowledge, positioning yourself as the essential bridge before the battle goes public.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: You have saved $30,000 to either pay down your mortgage principal aggressively or to start a side consulting business while keeping your day job — doing both halfway risks achieving neither goal.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: Paying down the mortgage guarantees a 6.5% effective return and lowers monthly overhead permanently, while the business might yield 20%+ but could also fail entirely. | Your current job is stable but stagnant; the consulting niche you've identified has a 2-3 year window before the market saturates. | Your spouse prefers the security of lower debt, while you feel the weight of an unrewarding career trajectory with no other obvious exit.
ANGLES:
1) [CONCENTRATE ON CERTAINTY] Put the full $30,000 toward the mortgage principal in a single lump sum, securing the guaranteed return, lowering monthly obligations, and strengthening your household's financial foundation before considering any venture.
2) [CONCENTRATE ON OPPORTUNITY] Commit the entire $30,000 to launching the consulting business at full capacity — branding, legal, a professional website, and a six-month runway of dedicated evening and weekend hours — accepting the mortgage paydown must wait.
3) [DIVIDE TO DE-RISK AND TEST] Split the capital: use $15,000 to make a meaningful extra mortgage payment that shortens the loan term, and use the other $15,000 to fund a lean, experimental version of the consulting business that validates demand without overcommitting.
4) [DIVIDE TO PRESERVE OPTIONALITY] Keep the $30,000 liquid in a high-yield account as a strategic reserve; pursue the consulting business using only sweat equity and a minimal $3,000 budget, preserving the bulk of capital to service the mortgage and maintain freedom to pivot if either path encounters trouble.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: Your scouts report dust clouds and stirrings in the mountain pass to the east, suggesting an enemy force of unknown size moving to flank your position, but the sightings are fragmented and could be a feint; if you commit your main force now you might crush a vulnerable column, yet if you wait for confirmation at dawn the pass may be lost and your army encircled.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: The dust clouds may signal a raiding party, a full division, or a deliberate deception using straw men to draw your army out of position. | Waiting until dawn will bring reliable light and returning scouts, but the enemy will have had a full night's march to seize the high ground. | Your own forces are well-rested and positioned defensively, but their supply line back to the province is exposed if the pass falls.
ANGLES:
1) [Calculated Offense] March immediately with two-thirds of your forces to strike the pass under moonlight; use the uncertainty as your ally, counting on speed and shock to break the enemy before he knows you are upon him.
2) [Patient Convergence] Hold your ground and send more scouts into the pass; await their return at dawn before any movement, accepting the risk of encirclement for the certainty of knowing the enemy's true strength and axis.
3) [Deceptive Shaping] Light numerous campfires along the western ridge and stage a loud, visible withdrawal of non-combatants; lure the enemy into revealing his intentions by making him believe you are retreating, then observe his response before committing.
4) [Positional Adjustment] Abandon your current camp and move the army to the heights around the waterfall ravine, a naturally strong position that guards both the supply line and the eastern approaches regardless of whether the pass threat is real or feigned, but cede the immediate initiative.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: You command a border garrison with barely enough strength to hold one pass. Scouts report a large army approaching the main pass in three days, while captured dispatches reveal a smaller elite raiding force will reach the undefended village pass in two days; you can reinforce one before either arrives, but not both.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: The main pass protects the kingdom's heartland and capital, where the king and court reside—its loss would be catastrophic. | The village pass guards the harvest stores and your soldiers' own families, including your second-in-command's entire clan. | The captured dispatches may be authentic or a planted deception; the larger army could be a feint while the real killing blow comes through the village.
ANGLES:
1) [Defend the center of gravity] March all available strength to the main pass immediately. The kingdom's survival outweighs local losses, and if the capital falls, the villages are doomed regardless. Prepare the main pass defenses and send riders ordering the village to evacuate into the hills, accepting that the harvest will be lost.
2) [Protect what is immediately precious] Rush the garrison to the village pass. The elite raiders will arrive first and they will show no mercy to the families there. Hold that ground, then force-march back toward the main pass hoping to delay the larger army's advance through skirmishing and terrain, buying time for reinforcements from the capital.
3) [Collapse the dilemma through intelligence] Send your fastest scouts in force toward both approaches with orders not to observe passively but to capture enemy scouts alive for interrogation. The captured dispatches are suspicious—verify which force is the true killing strike before committing. Meanwhile, split your best archers to slow both advances for one day while you confirm.
4) [Divide and harass, committing to neither] Split the garrison into two mobile detachments, neither strong enough for a fixed defense but each capable of setting ambushes, triggering rockslides, and burning bridges along both approach routes. The goal is not to stop either force but to slow both until the king can send relief, accepting that your families and the capital both face heightened risk for a time.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: Your three-year-old health-tech startup is losing $8k/month just as the market shifts radically toward AI-driven solutions; your co-founder secures an angel round and wants to double down, but you must now decide whether to commit further, pivot, merge, or walk away.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: Sunk cost of $200k and three years of life invested, making exit feel like public defeat. | The core product is suddenly a legacy offering as AI competitors begin winning your pilot customers. | Your co-founder and team of eight are looking to you for a decisive signal—delay itself is becoming a liability.
ANGLES:
1) [CUT LOSSES AND WITHDRAW] Wind down operations, protect remaining capital, and transition to a stable corporate role to preserve reputation and future optionality.
2) [REINFORCE AND ESCALATE] Accept the angel round, double marketing spend, and push through the headwind to capture enough market share before the AI shift fully consolidates.
3) [PIVOT AND TRANSFORM] Immediately retool the product to embed AI features, sacrificing short-term revenue to reposition the business inside the emergent trend.
4) [COALITION AND SHARE RISK] Merge with the strongest AI-driven competitor in your niche, exchanging equity for survival, and combine resources so neither side fights alone.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: Your army has surrounded a rival lord's last forces in a steep mountain pass, their food nearly gone. A direct assault will annihilate them but at great cost in your own men, as desperate foes fight hardest; yet if you relax the siege, they may escape to fight another season.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: The enemy commander is a cunning veteran who once slipped your grasp; his men still revere him. | Your own troops are exhausted from forced marches, and winter storms will soon isolate the pass. | Your lord demands the rebellion be crushed immediately but also quietly values preserving his core regiments for future campaigns.
ANGLES:
1) [Total Annihilation] Press the assault at dawn with overwhelming force, accepting heavy losses to destroy the enemy utterly and end the threat forever.
2) [Calculated Release] Open a narrow escape path, then harry their fleeing columns with light cavalry, wearing them down to a lesser victory that spares your infantry.
3) [Deceptive Ambush] Pretend to offer a safe route through a side gorge, but position archers and rocks on the heights to slaughter them mid-flight without a standing battle.
4) [Honorable Surrender] Send an envoy with generous terms: lay down weapons and the common soldiers go free, officers may ransom themselves, avoiding bloodshed and gaining a reputation for mercy that weakens future foes.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: Your main competitor abruptly folds, leaving their premium clients unserved. Seizing them could overload your small team and risk your reputation, but passing might allow rivals to lock you out of the market forever.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: Capacity strain on your team and possible burnout from sudden scaling | Risk of reputation damage if service quality drops due to overextension | Narrow time window before other agencies absorb the displaced clients
ANGLES:
1) [AGGRESSIVE EXPANSION] Immediately pursue all displaced clients, hire freelancers, and take on debt to scale overnight, accepting the risk of quality dips to capture the market.
2) [MEASURED CAPTURE] Quietly approach only the two clients that best fit your strengths, offering bespoke service without expanding headcount, leaving the rest to competitors.
3) [DEFENSIVE FORTIFICATION] Ignore the opening, instead lock in your existing clients with long-term contracts and enhanced service to withstand any competitive turbulence.
4) [TALENT ACQUISITION] Move swiftly to recruit the competitor's top-performing staff, strengthening your future capabilities rather than overextending now.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: Your thriving neighborhood bakery faces a direct threat: a well-funded national bakery chain is opening across the street next month. You must decide how to secure your livelihood without burning out in an unwinnable price war.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: Brand loyalty and deep community ties (your baked goods are cherished local staples, but chain convenience is unfamiliar) | Financial cushion and debt load (modest savings let you lose money briefly, but a prolonged price war would deplete everything) | Time pressure and opening momentum (the chain’s grand opening in four weeks will lock in early adopters unless you act now)
ANGLES:
1) [Direct Competitive Response] Launch a loyalty card, limited-time specials, and a neighborhood marketing blitz to defend your customer base head-on.
2) [Collaborative Alliance] Approach the chain manager with a proposal to become their exclusive local specialty supplier, turning a rival into a partner.
3) [Preemptive Dissuasion] Mobilize community petitions and local media to frame the chain’s entry as a threat to neighborhood character, making the location a reputational risk they'd rather avoid.
4) [Strategic Niche Repositioning] Pivot entirely to custom celebration cakes and event catering, abandoning the walk-in coffee-and-pastry market to the chain and owning a distinct, defensible space.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: You’ve spotted an unmet market need that aligns perfectly with your expertise, but entering now means revealing the opportunity to a much larger, well-resourced competitor who is known to crush new entrants. You could move first, let them commit elsewhere and counter, or shape the terrain so they choose not to fight at all.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: Timing: The market window may close within 6–12 months regardless of who enters, so delay itself carries mortal risk. | Resource asymmetry: Your advantage is speed and deep niche insight; their advantage is capital, distribution, and brand trust. | Competitor intent: If they see validated traction, they will enter—but if they enter without pre-existing commitment elsewhere, they will overwhelm you.
ANGLES:
1) [Preemptive Occupation] Launch quietly to a tightly defined, 'uninteresting' segment of the market and secure lock-in contracts or switching costs before the competitor notices. Your goal is to make the niche economically unattractive for them to invade, not to win a public race.
2) [Shaped Bait and Withdraw] Make a visible, modest move to trigger their premature entry into a suboptimal version of the space—then immediately pivot your real effort to an adjacent, more defensible segment they are now too distracted to pursue.
3) [Deliberate Second-Mover Counter] Hold completely still and let them launch first into a broad, capital-intensive approach. Study their weaknesses in execution, then enter surgically with a refined, cost-efficient offering that exploits the gaps they cannot easily close.
4) [Alliance-Based Containment] Rapidly form a coalition with two other small players who share the threat. Pool resources to present a unified substitute offering that raises the cost of entry for the competitor, making the fight not worth it for them while you each retain independence.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: As commander of a border garrison with only 3,000 men, you face an invading army of 15,000 advancing through the mountain pass that is the last defensible ground before your kingdom's capital. Your allies may send reinforcements within five days, but the enemy will reach the pass by dawn. Do you hold the pass now, forfeit the position to preserve the army, lure the enemy into a deadly ambush, or abandon the pass to strike their exposed supply columns?

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: The pass is the only terrain where your smaller force can neutralize the enemy's numerical advantage; once lost, the capital lies vulnerable on open plains. | The enemy is deep in unfamiliar territory, far from their supply base, and their troops are showing signs of fatigue and low morale after a forced march. | Reinforcements from your ally are promised but the messenger has not returned; they may arrive in three days, or not at all.
ANGLES:
1) [Fortification and Direct Engagement] Dig in at the pass, strengthen barricades, and force the enemy to assault your prepared position. Use the terrain to bleed them dry before your army is overwhelmed, buying precious time for relief forces.
2) [Strategic Withdrawal] Abandon the pass under cover of darkness, retreat into the wooded highlands, and avoid battle entirely. Trade space for time—let fatigue, hunger, and winter weather erode the enemy’s strength while preserving your own force intact.
3) [Deceptive Ambush] Stage a false retreat with a small rear guard, drawing the eager enemy into a narrow defile. Spring a concealed flanking ambush to shatter their vanguard and sow confusion, forcing the larger army to stall or retreat in disorder.
4) [Indirect Maneuver and Supply Disruption] Leave a skeleton force to man the pass and delay with parley and skirmishes. Move your main body by hidden mountain trails to strike the enemy’s unprotected baggage train and supply line, compelling them to turn back or starve before reaching the capital.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: Your army holds the high pass and must either advance to the fertile valley that feeds the enemy capital, or withdraw to the iron mines that arm your own forces—the enemy is divided between both, and you cannot hold the pass and also commit fully to one objective before winter closes the roads.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: Seizing the valley starves the enemy heartland and may force a surrender without a pitched battle, but stretches your supply line across hostile terrain. | Securing the mines guarantees your smiths can equip reinforcements due in spring, yet leaves the enemy's granaries untouched and buys them a winter to regroup. | Abandoning the pass entirely to strike either objective invites a third rival lord to occupy it and cut off your retreat, transforming a two-front calculation into a three-sided trap.
ANGLES:
1) [SEIZE THE OPPONENT'S STRENGTH] March on the valley immediately, living off captured granaries, and force the enemy to fight on ground you choose—parched and desperate—before the first snow.
2) [FORTIFY YOUR OWN STRENGTH] Withdraw to the mines in good order, leave a skeleton guard to collapse the pass behind you, and spend winter arming every able body so spring's army is an avalanche they cannot match.
3) [DECEIVE AND DIVIDE] Send a fast decoy force toward the valley with banners and night fires to fix the enemy's main body there, while the bulk of your army takes the mines in a lightning march, then offers the rival lord the pass as a gift to buy his neutrality through winter.
4) [REFUSE THE BINARY] Hold the pass and send raiding columns to both objectives—enough to burn the valley's outer granaries and carry away a season's ore—accepting that neither will be fully yours, but ensuring the enemy and rival lord both enter winter weaker than you.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: Your startup has runway for 12 months. You can bet everything on perfecting one flagship feature to dominate a niche, or you can launch three half-finished features to attract a broader user base—but you lack the resources to do both well.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.
Format exactly:
FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: Cash zero-date is hard: the 12-month runway is not a metaphor—if revenue doesn't cover costs by then, the company dies. | Early-adopter feedback shows equal pull for all four features, but engineering says polishing all three simultaneously guarantees none will be truly excellent. | A well-funded competitor is rumored to enter your space in 9 months; they could crush a broad-but-shallow product, but a deep niche might be too narrow for them to bother copying.
ANGLES:
1) [Concentrate force on the decisive point] Pick the one feature with the highest willingness-to-pay among early users, cut the other two ruthlessly, and deliver best-in-class depth to make the niche unassailable before the competitor arrives.
2) [Divide to threaten multiple fronts] Ship all three as a lightweight suite immediately, use broad adoption to gather real usage data, then let the market vote with behavior—doubling down later on whichever feature shows the steepest traction curve.
3) [Shape the battlefield via positioning] Launch the shallow suite but position it publicly as an 'integrated toolkit' solving a workflow the competitor's rumored single-point solution cannot address, forcing them to fight on a wider front where their resources spread thin.
4) [Withdraw to preserve the option to engage later] Release nothing new yet; extend runway by cutting all non-essential spend, run structured paid discovery pilots with 30 high-intent customers to validate exact willingness-to-pay per feature, and delay the build decision until the data is dispositive—accepting the risk of a later launch.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

PROBLEM: Outnumbered three to one, you hold a mountain pass against a rival general whose army masses for a thrust; he now requests a parley, while beyond both your banners a ruthless third power stirs. You must set your posture before the meeting, knowing your choice will decide the season's campaign.

FACETS: The rival's character is unclear—some scouts report a pragmatic expansionist, others a lord who prizes honour and stability. | Your position is nearly impregnable to direct assault, but your granaries are low and a prolonged siege would starve your garrison before relief can arrive. | A savage third state is pushing westward, a threat that could either crush both of you separately or forge an unexpected common cause.
ANGLES:
1) [Deception: Feigned Strength] Conduct vigorous drills, double your watch-fires, and let false reports of incoming reinforcements slip to the envoy—making the rival believe attacking would be too costly, thus buying time without shedding blood.
2) [Deception: Feigned Weakness] Pull back your forward pickets, leave a supply cart 'abandoned', and empty a section of wall—luring the rival into a hasty advance through the narrow defile where your hidden reserves can annihilate his vanguard.
3) [Openness: Common-Interest Alliance] Lay out your maps honestly at the parley, reveal the third power's encroachment, and propose a mutual defense pact—sharing intelligence and combining forces so that two potential enemies become a single allied line.
4) [Openness: Honourable Capitulation] Instruct your envoy to admit your garrison's true weakness openly, offer a symbolic tribute, and request a face-saving corridor of withdrawal—appealing to the rival's sense of righteousness and the moral burden of slaughtering a lesser foe who offers no resistance.

FACETS: Time erodes advantage as surely as enemy action—the pass is the only corridor through which your supply lines can be threatened. | Your scouts are reliable but divided: one swears the tracks indicate a full brigade, another insists the depth suggests only a decoy vanguard. | Committing reserves now leaves your camp vulnerable to a secondary thrust from the eastern road, which intelligence suggested was inactive but cannot be verified.
ANGLES:
1) [Exploit Tempo (Strike Now)] Seize the advantage of surprise—march immediately with your best troops and hit the pass before the enemy consolidates, accepting the risk of ambush as the price of denying them the ground.
2) [Shape the Terrain (Delay and Maneuver)] Do not wait idly—dispatch a screening force to the pass to harass and fix any emerging enemy while you reposition your main body to more defensible high ground, turning the fog into a shared liability.
3) [Test Before Committing (Probe)] Send a small, fast detachment into the fog with strict orders to engage lightly, gather intelligence, and retreat; let their report—or their silence—clarify the enemy's true strength before you stake your army.
4) [Preserve Strength (Strategic Withdrawal)] Accept that the pass may be compromised and consolidate your forces around the supply depot to the south, forcing the enemy to emerge from the mountains into open ground where you can fight on your terms, with certainty of their position.

3) [Feint] Publicly announce a different, less powerful analytics product as a 'new initiative,' leading the rival to underestimate your real capability. While they react to the decoy, quietly perfect the real tool for a decisive blow.
4) [Alliance] Privately share the tool’s existence with a complementary non-competing firm (e.g., a data provider) to form a strategic partnership. Use their resources to accelerate scaling, and let the partnership’s implied new power deter the rival indirectly without you ever showing your hand directly.

PROBLEM: Your garrison of 600 holds a vital river ford. An enemy force of 2,000 approaches at dawn. Your scouts report they are exhausted and low on grain, but their commander is cautious. You must decide how to present your strength before their emissary arrives at midday—reveal your true numbers, feign greater strength, appear pitifully weak, or simply refuse the parley—each path shapes whether battle comes and on what terms.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.

FACETS: The enemy’s true condition is uncertain—your scouts may be wrong, and the exhaustion could be a ruse to draw you out. | Your own supplies can last ten days; reinforcement is at least eight days away, making every day of delay precious. | The river is low this season, fordable in three places within a mile—you cannot hold all crossings simultaneously with 600 men.
ANGLES:
1) [APPEAR STRONG] Double the cooking fires, have the same companies march in circles through the tree line raising dust, and dress camp followers in spare armor along the ridge. When the emissary arrives, receive him amidst a show of bustling, well-fed confidence. If he believes you hold 2,000, he will offer terms rather than risk assault.
2) [APPEAR WEAK] Conceal half your men in the ravine, leave tents half-empty, and have visible soldiers slouch at posts with untended weapons. Let the emissary report a force of 300 unprepared farmers. When the enemy advances carelessly at night, spring the ambush from the flank and crush their vanguard in the river shallows.
3) [REFUSE THE PARLEY] Send the emissary back without audience, shooting arrows at his horse’s feet. The insult will either enrage their commander into a rash attack—denying him the rest he desperately needs—or make him fear a trap so greatly that he halts for days to gather intelligence, buying your reinforcements precious time.
4) [DEAL OPENLY] Receive the emissary with plain truth: lay out your exact numbers, your prepared positions, and your willingness to fight to the last man. Offer him food for his hungry troops and safe passage across the ford—if they march on without lingering. An exhausted army, shown both the certainty of heavy loss and an honorable path forward, may choose to conserve its strength for battles it cannot avoid.

ANGLES:
1) [Decisive Concentration] March all forces north immediately. Strike the enemy’s vanguard before it can deploy, using terrain to neutralize their numbers. A shattered main threat will awe the rebellious city and scatter the bandits.
2) [Internal Pacification] Detach a fast column to the eastern city, publicly execute the rebel leaders, and install a loyal garrison. A secure heartland prevents cascading chaos and frees attention later for the other fronts.
3) [Resource Fortification] Abandon exposed outposts. Concentrate on fortifying the western passes with mobile patrols to keep supply lines open. A fed army can outlast any siege; bandits are a knife at the throat that must be removed first.
4) [Dilatory Dispersion] Send small, deceptive contingents to each front: a show of force to the north to delay with false campfires, a negotiator to the eastern city to buy days, and light cavalry to harass the bandits. Meanwhile, prepare a central reserve to strike wherever the situation deteriorates most.

Marcus must decide before dawn whether to disclose the coming relief, keep silent and secure the profit, delay the transaction, or sell instead to the starving public.

FACETS: <f1> | <f2> | <f3>
ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: The tension between honestas (honorable conduct) and utile (expediency): profiting from another's ignorance versus the duty to provide for one's household and creditors. | The uncertainty of the relief ships' arrival—a storm, wreck, or delay could mean the senator's ignorance is not a hidden defect but a genuine market risk, so disclosure might sacrifice certain gain for a phantom good. | The buyer is no anonymous trader but a senator whose future goodwill or enmity carries political and legal weight, making this a test of long-term reputation as much as a single transaction.
ANGLES:
1) [Full disclosure] Inform the senator openly about the incoming ships, accept whatever price he then offers, and trust that honesty builds lasting trust and serves the honorable path.
2) [Silent sale within the law] Proceed with the sale, answer only what is asked, and make no false statements; treat the transaction as legitimate commerce where caveat emptor rules and family duty justifies keeping the intelligence private.
3) [Strategic delay] Postpone the deal under a credible pretext (such as needing to inspect the grain's condition) until the new ships arrive, letting circumstances naturally align price with truth without overt deception or immediate financial sacrifice.
4) [Civic redistribution] Decline the senator's private offer and instead sell the grain openly to the people at a moderate ‘just price,’ preferring public honor and the gratitude of the many over a single lucrative deal with a powerful man.

This gives the caregiver immediate title and the others immediate cash, but requires financial risk-sharing and agreement on valuation.
4) [Shared equity enterprise] Retain joint ownership and convert the house into a rental property with the caregiver as tenant paying manageable rent; split net rental income among all heirs. This preserves the asset as an investment, keeps the caregiver in place, and defers the sale decision, but demands ongoing cooperation and property management.

4) [REFUSE THE DICHOTOMY (the reframing path)] Neither follow Alcibiades nor retreat to the workshop; instead, seek a different patron among the cautious faction—Nicias or another—who would value your martial reputation but pursue a defensive strategy, allowing you to serve Athens while staying near home.

ANGLES:

2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: His deep longing for citizenship for himself and his sons clashes with the Athenian ideal of moderate self-restraint (sophrosyne). | Accepting singular credit could provoke divine phthonos, while rejecting the city's gift might insult the democratic will and seem ungrateful. | Some citizens already grumble that a foreigner's elevation disturbs the natural order, so any stance will shape his family's enduring social integration.
ANGLES:
1) [Assertive Reception] Accept both citizenship and crown without qualification, delivering a speech that openly claims the achievement as the fruit of your own foresight and industry, and treat the honors as just recognition of excellence.
2) [Pious Deference] Accept the citizenship for your household, but dedicate the golden crown on the altar of Athena Polias, publicly attributing the fleet's safe arrival to divine favor rather than human merit, thereby tempering pride with piety.
3) [Self-Restraining Refusal] Politely decline all formal honors, insisting that you merely acted as any decent member of the community would, that your reward is having helped, and that you wish to remain the unadorned metic you have always been.
4) [Communal Dispersion] Accept citizenship for yourself, but petition the Assembly to revoke the individual crown and instead bestow a collective honor on the grain-market overseers and ship captains who labored beside you, dispersing the acclaim among many hands.

PROBLEM: Nicostratus, a wealthy Athenian, promised a reward of 100 minas to his three servants for saving his life; his will says to divide it 'according to their merit.' One cured him of a deadly illness, another risked his life to rescue him from a fire, and the third uncovered a murderous plot. The executor must now interpret 'merit' and distribute the sum without alienating any servant.

Give the three key FACETS any complete answer must engage, then FOUR genuinely distinct strategic ANGLES for approaching the whole problem. Each angle must be a categorically different KIND of move (a distinct family) leading to a different action — sharp alternatives, not rephrasings.

ANGLES:
1) [FAMILY] <angle>
2) [FAMILY] <angle>
3) [FAMILY] <angle>
4) [FAMILY] <angle>

FACETS: The phrase 'according to their merit' is ambiguous: it could refer to the value of the service rendered, the virtue displayed, the effort and sacrifice made, or the overall contribution to the master's well-being. | The three servants performed incommensurable acts—medical skill, physical courage, and intelligence—so no single measure of worth is obvious. | The executor must maintain harmonious relations with all three, each of whom strongly believes his own contribution was indispensable, while also upholding a reputation for just judgment.
ANGLES:
1) [ARITHMETIC EQUALITY] Divide the 100 minas into three equal parts. In friendship and gratitude, each servant’s act was equally essential to saving the master, so equal shares reflect a just return of honor.
2) [PROPORTIONAL BY SERVICE VALUE] Assess the monetary worth each service would command on the market. Hire a physician would cost X, a bodyguard’s risk Y, a private informer’s intelligence Z; then distribute the 100 minas in exact proportion to these values.
3) [PROPORTIONAL BY VIRTUE] Rank the servants by the moral excellence of their acts. The physical rescuer displayed the highest courage, thus deserves the largest share; the physician’s skill is admirable but less noble; the informer’s cunning is least virtuous and so receives the smallest portion.
4) [PROPORTIONAL BY NEED] Consider the current circumstances of each servant. Allocate the reward so that the one who is aged and impoverished receives enough to live out his days in comfort, while the younger, prosperous ones receive proportionally less, aiming at the shared end of a life well-lived for all.
You refract a hard decision problem into four categorically distinct strategic angles.

FACETS: The insult was ambiguous enough that some may dismiss it as rhetorical bluster, while others will hold it as a serious stain unless answered. | Your rival holds key votes in the assembly and his support is needed to pass a defense decree that the city urgently requires against a looming external threat. | Any overt retaliation risks escalating into a feud that could divide the citizen body and undermine your own standing as a calm, deliberative leader.
ANGLES:
1) [Direct Private Confrontation] Seek out the rival immediately after the assembly, alone or with a trusted witness. 2) [Civic-Legal Redress] Bring a formal charge of slander (kakegoria) before the magistrates. Let the law examine the truth of the accusation in a rational forum, turning the insult into a matter of evidence and civic procedure. This upholds order and deflects the charge that you act from passion.
3) [Magnanimous Forbearance] Publicly ignore the remark entirely, treating it as beneath your notice. In the next assembly, speak only of the common good and praise the rival’s past services, reframing the narrative around unity. This displays supreme self-control and puts the city’s survival above personal slight, shaming the aggressor by contrast.
4) [Irony and Oratorical Jujitsu] In your next public address, make no direct accusation, but use wit and feigned humility to turn the insult into a source of laughter at your rival’s expense. Suggest that if such baseless tales are the best he can offer, his judgment must be as weak as his evidence. This reclaims honour through cleverness without overt conflict, winning the crowd's sympathy.

2) [Test by adversity] Deliberately simulate a crisis—a false report of a border incursion or a fabricated diplomatic insult from a rival state—and observe how each advisor counsels action under pressure. Retain only those who demonstrate judgment and candor when stakes appear grave, publicly rewarding their prudence afterward to legitimize the purge as merit-based.
3) [Delegated triage] Empower a single, unimpeachable adjudicator—a respected elder statesman of known integrity but no independent power base—to interview both factions, review confidential assessments, and recommend a final slate. The prince accepts the recommendations unchanged, absorbing neither blame nor gratitude directly, while keeping a few of the dismissed close as ‘honored companions’ to monitor their discontent.
4) [Holding to tradition with structured dissent] Retain the old guard as the visible Council of State, satisfying custom and their honor, but publicly appoint one or two of the most brilliant young courtiers as ‘Required Objectors’ whose formal duty is to challenge every major proposal in writing before enactment. This channels flattery into intellectual combat, tests the old counselors’ reasoning, and splits the young between loyal gadflies and those who will overreach and expose themselves.
